data.content:
Benchmarks complete. Here are the results (10,000-word corpus):

```
| Method              | Runtime   |    Mean |  Allocated |
|-------------------- |---------- |--------:|-----------:|
| AverageWordLength   | .NET 10.0 | 30.8 μs |        0 B |
| WordLengthHistogram | .NET 10.0 | 43.1 μs |      136 B |
| UniqueWordCount     | .NET 10.0 | 400.5 μs |    1,552 B |
| AverageWordLength   | .NET 9.0  | 46.9 μs |        0 B |
| WordLengthHistogram | .NET 9.0  | 93.7 μs |      136 B |
| UniqueWordCount     | .NET 9.0  | 342.3 μs |    1,552 B |
```

**Summary:**
- **AverageWordLength**: ~34% faster on .NET 10 (47→31 μs)
- **WordLengthHistogram**: ~54% faster on .NET 10 (94→43 μs)
- **UniqueWordCount**: ~17% slower on .NET 10 (342→401 μs), though note the .NET 10 runs had high variance/outliers — the machine was likely under load, making these numbers noisy. Memory allocations are identical across both runtimes.

⚠️ BenchmarkDotNet flagged bimodal distribution and removed many outliers for the .NET 10 runs, suggesting external load interference. The .NET 9 results were very stable (tiny StdDev). Take the .NET 10 absolute numbers with a grain of salt, but the `AverageWordLength` and `WordLengthHistogram` improvements appear genuine.
